explicit_text: I started my longshore career in 1967 after returning from Alaska. I was a member of the ILWU Local 32 and 52, and over the years, I worked on log ships and other cargo. The union was very important to me, especially during events like the 1971 ILWU strike and the Everett massacre. I eventually became involved in union leadership roles, including business manager and welfare director.

explicit_text: I was raised in Everett, Washington by my aunt and uncle, who were Greek immigrants. Everett was a mill town with a strong union presence, which shaped my early life and community. I attended Everett High School and spent most of my life there, growing up in a union town with many mills and union families around me.

explicit_text: I moved to Anchorage, Alaska in 1964 to work in construction for my uncle's company. I worked in various places including Kenai, Big Lake, and Anchorage, building officers' quarters at Elmendorf Air Force Base and doing piledriving in Cook Inlet. It was a busy time in Alaska’s construction boom and my first experience with the Laborers' International Union of North America.

explicit_text: My first longshore job was on May 28, 1967, on the Kanoshima Maru, a log ship. It was a tough introduction to longshoring, working with logs that came in as they grew, and learning the ropes from experienced men. That first day was scary but it marked the start of my long and rewarding career on the waterfront.
